title: Willow Christmas star
subtitle: A five-pointed willow star wreath made from fresh or soaked rods
excerpt: A five-pointed star from willow rods takes less than an hour and requires no tools beyond a knife to trim ends. Five rods are bent and woven through each other in sequence to form the star points, with the weave locking every rod in place without tying. This beginner project works with fresh-cut willow in winter or with dried buff willow soaked overnight.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Household Guide (1869) for willow wreath and decorative forms. Victorian Christmas craft tradition, widely documented in late-nineteenth-century domestic manuals.

glossaryTerms.definition: Dried, bark-stripped willow rod. Buff in colour, flexible after soaking. Available from basket-making suppliers; the most consistent material for decorative willow work.
Fresh-cut willow rod used within a few days of cutting. Flexible without soaking but will dry and shrink slightly in the finished form.

content.text: This willow star uses five rods woven through each other in a pentagonal pattern; the weave locks every rod in place through friction and the natural spring tension of the willow. It works with fresh-cut
green willow
from a winter cut, or with dried
buff willow
rods soaked overnight. Five matching rods give a symmetrical star; slightly varying diameters still produce a usable result and are acceptable for a first attempt.
What you need: materials

Five willow rods, each 60 to 80 cm long, 5 to 7 mm in diameter, as similar in size as possible. Green willow (cut within 3 days) works without soaking. Dried buff willow needs overnight soaking in cold water plus 30 minutes wrapped in a damp cloth before use. Thin florist's wire or string to secure the hanging loop at the top point.
What you need: tools
A sloyd knife to trim rod ends flush. No other tools are needed.
Safety
Trim rod ends away from the body. Willow rod tips are sharp when freshly cut.
The work
Step 1: Bend the first rod into a V
Take the first rod and bend it in half to form a V shape. The bend at the midpoint becomes one point of the star. Lay the V flat on a surface with the open end toward you.
Step 2: Weave in the remaining rods

Take the second rod and thread it through the V of the first, crossing it over one arm and under the other. Bend the second rod to its own V point at the midpoint and position it 72 degrees around the imaginary pentagon from the first point. Continue with rods three, four, and five: each one threads through the previous V (over one arm, under the other) and forms its own point at 72-degree intervals around the star. The fifth rod threads both through its preceding V and back under the arm of the first rod to close the pentagon. Hold the tension by pressing each intersection with a thumb while threading the next rod.
Step 3: Adjust and tighten

Once all five rods are in place, slide each one along its length to adjust the star point proportions until all five points are equal in length. The friction of the interlocked rods holds the form without tying. Trim any excess rod length at the tips with the knife to make the points even. Loop a length of thin wire around the top point and twist to form a hanging loop.
Variations
A six-pointed star uses six rods in the same over-under sequence, spaced at 60-degree intervals. The locking of the sixth rod requires more tension than the five-rod version.
A cluster of three stars in different sizes, hung at different heights, makes a simple window decoration that dries in place over the first week without losing its shape.
Care
No care needed once dry. A green willow star may drip slightly in the first hour after making; hang it over a sink until dry.

items.symptom: The star will not hold its shape and collapses
items.cause: The fifth rod was not threaded back under the first rod's arm, leaving the pentagon open.
items.fix: Disassemble back to the fourth rod and re-thread the fifth, making sure it passes under the arm of the first rod at the closing intersection.

items.symptom: One or two points are much longer or shorter than the others
items.cause: The V bends were not positioned at the correct intervals before tightening.
items.fix: Slide each rod along its length through the interlocked intersections to rebalance the point lengths before the form fully sets on drying.
